quote: The lattice of values in LLVM is: immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze(poison) > concrete value. / It is only valid to transform values from the left to the right. / Since division triggers UB when the divisor is zero, we can only execute speculatively if we are sure we do not hit that condition.

nsw->poison half corroborated by the verified UB manual; wraps-modulo-2^n half could not be re-fetched verbatim (LangRef page exceeds fetch size on two attempts, including anchored). Not refuted; direct quote pending a sectioned source.

The Loop Vectorizer handles this loop by placing code that checks, at runtime, if the arrays A and B point to disjointed memory locations. If arrays A and B overlap, then the scalar version of the loop is executed. / LLVM supports vectorizing floating point reductions only when at least -fassociative-math -fno-signed-zeros -fno-trapping-math is used. / On some targets, such as AArch64 and RISC-V, LLVM can generate ordered reductions.

If there is a race on a given memory location, loads from that location return undef. / a store cannot be inserted along a path where it might not execute otherwise. / an unordered load or store cannot be split into multiple instructions.

any Go program that is data-race-free ... can only have outcomes explained by some sequentially consistent interleaving ... This property is called DRF-SC. / Any implementation can, upon detecting a data race, report the race and halt execution. / a compiler must not introduce writes that do not exist in the original program / it must not allow a single read to observe multiple values.

Since Function is an abstract type, every call to wrapper.f will require dynamic dispatch. / the inner loop can be recompiled as part of fill_twos! for different types. / if you have hundreds or thousands of such types ... every function will have hundreds or thousands of variants compiled for it.

by compiling different specializations Julia can do all the method lookup ahead of time. / ERROR: MethodError: g(::Float64, ::Float64) is ambiguous. / any new method definition will not be visible to the current runtime environment, including Tasks and Threads.
